subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Nomenclature: Aldehydes and Ketones Common names: replace -e from alkyl group by aldehyde or ketone e.g. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3^{-}{/tex} CHO is acetaldehyde. IUPAC names: replace -e by -al for aldehydes and -one for ketones. e.g. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3^{-}{/tex} CHO is ethanal and {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{COCH}_3{/tex} is propanone.
text_html:
Nomenclature: Aldehydes and Ketones
Common names: replace -e from alkyl group by aldehyde or ketone e.g. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3^{-}{/tex}CHO is acetaldehyde.
IUPAC names: replace -e by -al for aldehydes and -one for ketones. e.g.{tex}\mathrm{CH}_3^{-}{/tex}CHO is ethanal and {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{COCH}_3{/tex} is propanone.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Importance of Carbonyl Compounds Carbonyl compounds hold significant value in organic chemistry. - They are components of fabrics, flavorings, plastics, and pharmaceuticals.
text_html:
Importance of Carbonyl Compounds
Carbonyl compounds hold significant value in organic chemistry.
- They are components of fabrics, flavorings, plastics, and pharmaceuticals.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Carbonyl Group Description A carbonyl group consists of a carbon-oxygen double bond (>C=O). - It is a crucial functional group in organic chemistry.
text_html:
Carbonyl Group Description
A carbonyl group consists of a carbon-oxygen double bond (>C=O).
- It is a crucial functional group in organic chemistry.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Role in Nature and Industry Aldehydes, ketones, and carboxylic acids are ubiquitous in plants and animals. - They add pleasant fragrances, for example, vanillin and cinnamaldehyde. - Serve in food, pharmaceuticals, and as industrial solvents like acetone.
text_html:
Role in Nature and Industry
Aldehydes, ketones, and carboxylic acids are ubiquitous in plants and animals.
- They add pleasant fragrances, for example, vanillin and cinnamaldehyde.
- Serve in food, pharmaceuticals, and as industrial solvents like acetone.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Nomenclature of Aldehydes and Ketones (Common Names) - Aldehyde names derive from carboxylic acids, changing '-ic' to 'aldehyde'. - Ketones named using bonded alkyl groups, known commonly as acetone or phenone.
text_html:
Nomenclature of Aldehydes and Ketones (Common Names)
- Aldehyde names derive from carboxylic acids, changing '-ic' to 'aldehyde'.
- Ketones named using bonded alkyl groups, known commonly as acetone or phenone.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Nomenclature (IUPAC Names) - Replace '-e' in alkanes with '-al' for aldehydes and '-one' for ketones. - Aldehyde numbering starts at the carbonyl group's carbon. - Ketone numbering begins from closest end to the carbonyl.
text_html:
Nomenclature (IUPAC Names)
- Replace '-e' in alkanes with '-al' for aldehydes and '-one' for ketones.
- Aldehyde numbering starts at the carbonyl group's carbon.
- Ketone numbering begins from closest end to the carbonyl.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Structure of the Carbonyl Group The carbonyl carbon is sp 2 -hybridized and forms sigma (σ) bonds. - π-bond with oxygen and lies planar with attached atoms.
text_html:
Structure of the Carbonyl Group
The carbonyl carbon is sp2-hybridized and forms sigma (σ) bonds.
- π-bond with oxygen and lies planar with attached atoms.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Bonding and Polarity Polarization of carbon-oxygen bond due to oxygen's higher electronegativity. - Carbonyl carbon as an electrophilic center; oxygen as nucleophilic.
text_html:
Bonding and Polarity
Polarization of carbon-oxygen bond due to oxygen's higher electronegativity.
- Carbonyl carbon as an electrophilic center; oxygen as nucleophilic.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Common and IUPAC Names Common and IUPAC names of aldehydes and ketones differ. - The common names are rooted in original sources of acids.
text_html:
Common and IUPAC Names
Common and IUPAC names of aldehydes and ketones differ.
- The common names are rooted in original sources of acids.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Dipole Moment Carbonyl compounds have significant dipole moments because of their polar nature. - Explained via resonance forms, both neutral and dipolar structures.
text_html:
Dipole Moment
Carbonyl compounds have significant dipole moments because of their polar nature.
- Explained via resonance forms, both neutral and dipolar structures.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones Aldehydes and ketones are commonly prepared by oxidation of primary and secondary alcohols, respectively.
text_html:
Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
Aldehydes and ketones are commonly prepared by oxidation of primary and secondary alcohols, respectively.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Dehydrogenation of Alcohols Volatile alcohols are dehydrogenated in industrial applications using metal catalysts like Ag or Cu to form aldehydes from primary alcohols and ketones from secondary alcohols.
text_html:
Dehydrogenation of Alcohols
Volatile alcohols are dehydrogenated in industrial applications using metal catalysts like Ag or Cu to form aldehydes from primary alcohols and ketones from secondary alcohols.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Preparation from Hydrocarbons - Ozonolysis of Alkenes Ozonolysis of alkenes, followed by treatment with zinc dust and water, produces aldehydes, ketones, or both, depending on the substitution pattern of the alkene.
text_html:
Preparation from Hydrocarbons - Ozonolysis of Alkenes
Ozonolysis of alkenes, followed by treatment with zinc dust and water, produces aldehydes, ketones, or both, depending on the substitution pattern of the alkene.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Preparation from Hydrocarbons - Hydration of Alkynes The addition of water to ethyne with H2SO4 and HgSO4 results in acetaldehyde, whereas other alkynes yield ketones.
text_html:
Preparation from Hydrocarbons - Hydration of Alkynes
The addition of water to ethyne with H2SO4 and HgSO4 results in acetaldehyde, whereas other alkynes yield ketones.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Preparation of Aldehydes from Acyl Chlorides Acyl chlorides can be hydrogenated over palladium on barium sulfate to form aldehydes via the Rosenmund reduction.
text_html:
Preparation of Aldehydes from Acyl Chlorides
Acyl chlorides can be hydrogenated over palladium on barium sulfate to form aldehydes via the Rosenmund reduction.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Stephen Reaction for Aldehydes Nitriles are reduced to imines with stannous chloride and HCl, and subsequent hydrolysis yields aldehydes in the Stephen reaction.
text_html:
Stephen Reaction for Aldehydes
Nitriles are reduced to imines with stannous chloride and HCl, and subsequent hydrolysis yields aldehydes in the Stephen reaction.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Etard Reaction Chromyl chloride oxidizes methylbenzene to benzaldehyde by forming a chromium complex that is hydrolyzed.
text_html:
Etard Reaction
Chromyl chloride oxidizes methylbenzene to benzaldehyde by forming a chromium complex that is hydrolyzed.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Commercial Benzaldehyde Production Benzylic chlorination followed by hydrolysis of toluene yields benzaldehyde.
text_html:
Commercial Benzaldehyde Production
Benzylic chlorination followed by hydrolysis of toluene yields benzaldehyde.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Friedel-Crafts Acylation Aldehydes are prepared from benzene or substituted benzenes using acyl chloride and anhydrous aluminium chloride.
text_html:
Friedel-Crafts Acylation
Aldehydes are prepared from benzene or substituted benzenes using acyl chloride and anhydrous aluminium chloride.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Preparation of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Ketones from Acyl Chlorides Reach ketones through acyl chlorides treatment with dialkylcadmium, prepared with cadmium chloride and Grignard reagent.
text_html:
Ketones from Acyl Chlorides
Reach ketones through acyl chlorides treatment with dialkylcadmium, prepared with cadmium chloride and Grignard reagent.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Physical States of Aldehydes and Ketones - Methanal is a gas at room temperature. - Ethanal is a volatile liquid. - Most other aldehydes and ketones are either liquids or solids at room temperature.
text_html:
Physical States of Aldehydes and Ketones
- Methanal is a gas at room temperature.
- Ethanal is a volatile liquid.
- Most other aldehydes and ketones are either liquids or solids at room temperature.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Boiling Points Order Boiling points are affected by molecular mass and types of interactions: Aldehydes and ketones have higher boiling points than hydrocarbons due to dipole-dipole interactions. Their boiling points are lower than alcohols due to lack of hydrogen bonding.
text_html:
Boiling Points Order
Boiling points are affected by molecular mass and types of interactions:
  • Aldehydes and ketones have higher boiling points than hydrocarbons due to dipole-dipole interactions.
  • Their boiling points are lower than alcohols due to lack of hydrogen bonding.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Ranking Boiling Points Molecular mass and boiling points are compared as follows: n-Butane (273 K, molecular mass 58) Methoxyethane (281 K, molecular mass 60) Propanal (322 K, molecular mass 58) Acetone (329 K, molecular mass 58) Propan-1-ol (370 K, molecular mass 60)
text_html:
Ranking Boiling Points
Molecular mass and boiling points are compared as follows:
  1. n-Butane (273 K, molecular mass 58)
  2. Methoxyethane (281 K, molecular mass 60)
  3. Propanal (322 K, molecular mass 58)
  4. Acetone (329 K, molecular mass 58)
  5. Propan-1-ol (370 K, molecular mass 60)

subject: Chemistry
topic: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Boiling Points Influence Dipole-dipole interactions result in higher boiling points in comparison to hydrocarbons and ethers.
text_html:
Boiling Points Influence
Dipole-dipole interactions result in higher boiling points in comparison to hydrocarbons and ethers.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Solubility in Water - Lower aldehydes and ketones (like methanal, ethanal, propanone) are highly miscible with water due to hydrogen bonding. - Solubility decreases with longer alkyl chains.
text_html:
Solubility in Water
- Lower aldehydes and ketones (like methanal, ethanal, propanone) are highly miscible with water due to hydrogen bonding.
- Solubility decreases with longer alkyl chains.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Solubility in Organic Solvents Aldehydes and ketones dissolve well in organic solvents like: Benzene Ether Methanol Chloroform
text_html:
Solubility in Organic Solvents
Aldehydes and ketones dissolve well in organic solvents like:
  • Benzene
  • Ether
  • Methanol
  • Chloroform

subject: Chemistry
topic: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Odor Characteristics - Lower aldehydes have a sharp, pungent odor. - As the molecular size increases, the odor becomes less pungent and more fragrant.
text_html:
Odor Characteristics
- Lower aldehydes have a sharp, pungent odor.
- As the molecular size increases, the odor becomes less pungent and more fragrant.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Use in Perfumery and Flavors Naturally occurring aldehydes and ketones are important in creating perfumes and food flavorings due to their fragrant nature.
text_html:
Use in Perfumery and Flavors
Naturally occurring aldehydes and ketones are important in creating perfumes and food flavorings due to their fragrant nature.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Interaction Properties Aldehydes and ketones are able to form weak molecular associations due to: Dipole-dipole interactions. Formation of hydrogen bonds with water (for lower ones).
text_html:
Interaction Properties
Aldehydes and ketones are able to form weak molecular associations due to:
  • Dipole-dipole interactions.
  • Formation of hydrogen bonds with water (for lower ones).

subject: Chemistry
topic: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Temperature and Physical State - At room temperature, methanal is gaseous, while ethanol is a liquid form. - Larger molecules among aldehydes and ketones may solidify due to intermolecular forces.
text_html:
Temperature and Physical State
- At room temperature, methanal is gaseous, while ethanol is a liquid form.
- Larger molecules among aldehydes and ketones may solidify due to intermolecular forces.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Structure of Carbonyl group Carbonyl carbon is sp 2 Hybridised and planar in shape, with one sigma and one {tex}\pi{/tex} bond between C = O
text_html:
Structure of Carbonyl group

Carbonyl carbon is sp2 Hybridised and planar in shape, with one sigma and one {tex}\pi{/tex} bond between C = O

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Nucleophilic Addition Reactions Nucleophilic addition reactions involve a nucleophile reacting with the carbonyl group. Aldehydes and ketones undergo these reactions, leading to the formation of various compounds. Aldehydes, due to less steric hindrance, are generally more reactive than ketones.
text_html: Nucleophilic Addition Reactions Nucleophilic addition reactions involve a nucleophile reacting with the carbonyl group. Aldehydes and ketones undergo these reactions, leading to the formation of various compounds. Aldehydes, due to less steric hindrance, are generally more reactive than ketones.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Mechanism of Nucleophilic Addition A nucleophile generally attacks the carbon atom of the carbonyl group perpendicularly to the plane of sp2 hybridized orbitals, forming a tetrahedral intermediate. Aldehydes shift from sp2 to sp3 hybridization and capture a proton to form a neutral product.
text_html: Mechanism of Nucleophilic Addition A nucleophile generally attacks the carbon atom of the carbonyl group perpendicularly to the plane of sp2 hybridized orbitals, forming a tetrahedral intermediate. Aldehydes shift from sp2 to sp3 hybridization and capture a proton to form a neutral product.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Reactivity of Aldehydes and Ketones Aldehydes exhibit higher reactivity compared to ketones in nucleophilic addition reactions. The steric effect of ketones' two substituents and the electronic effect of alkyl groups reduce the reactivity of ketones significantly.
text_html: Reactivity of Aldehydes and Ketones Aldehydes exhibit higher reactivity compared to ketones in nucleophilic addition reactions. The steric effect of ketones' two substituents and the electronic effect of alkyl groups reduce the reactivity of ketones significantly.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Examples of Nucleophilic Addition Prominent examples include: Addition of hydrogen cyanide (HCN) to form cyanohydrins. Addition of sodium hydrogensulphite resulting in soluble addition compounds. Grignard reagents forming secondary or tertiary alcohols. Addition of alcohols forming hemiacetals and acetals. Ammonia derivatives resulting in >C=N-Z compounds.
text_html:
Examples of Nucleophilic Addition Prominent examples include:
  • Addition of hydrogen cyanide (HCN) to form cyanohydrins.
  • Addition of sodium hydrogensulphite resulting in soluble addition compounds.
  • Grignard reagents forming secondary or tertiary alcohols.
  • Addition of alcohols forming hemiacetals and acetals.
  • Ammonia derivatives resulting in >C=N-Z compounds.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Reduction Reactions Aldehydes and ketones can be reduced to alcohols (aldehydes to primary alcohols, ketones to secondary) using reducing agents like sodium borohydride (NaBH4) or lithium aluminum hydride (LiAlH4). They can also be reduced to hydrocarbons with zinc amalgam or in the Wolff-Kishner reaction.
text_html: Reduction Reactions Aldehydes and ketones can be reduced to alcohols (aldehydes to primary alcohols, ketones to secondary) using reducing agents like sodium borohydride (NaBH4) or lithium aluminum hydride (LiAlH4). They can also be reduced to hydrocarbons with zinc amalgam or in the Wolff-Kishner reaction.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Oxidation Reactions Aldehydes oxidize easily to form carboxylic acids with agents like nitric acid or potassium permanganate. They can be distinguished from ketones by Tollens' and Fehling's tests. Methyl ketones exhibit oxidation through haloform reactions, producing carboxylic acids and haloform.
text_html: Oxidation Reactions Aldehydes oxidize easily to form carboxylic acids with agents like nitric acid or potassium permanganate. They can be distinguished from ketones by Tollens' and Fehling's tests. Methyl ketones exhibit oxidation through haloform reactions, producing carboxylic acids and haloform.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Reactions Due to α-Hydrogens α-hydrogens in carbonyl compounds are acidic because of the electron-withdrawing carbonyl group. This leads to reactions such as aldol condensation. Aldol condensation can be "cross," involving two different carbonyl compounds producing multiple products.
text_html: Reactions Due to α-Hydrogens α-hydrogens in carbonyl compounds are acidic because of the electron-withdrawing carbonyl group. This leads to reactions such as aldol condensation. Aldol condensation can be "cross," involving two different carbonyl compounds producing multiple products.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Aldol Condensation In the presence of dilute alkali, carbonyl compounds having at least one α-hydrogen undergo aldol reactions producing β-hydroxy aldehydes or ketones, which can dehydrate to α, β-unsaturated carbonyl compounds.
text_html: Aldol Condensation In the presence of dilute alkali, carbonyl compounds having at least one α-hydrogen undergo aldol reactions producing β-hydroxy aldehydes or ketones, which can dehydrate to α, β-unsaturated carbonyl compounds.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Cannizzaro Reaction Aldehydes without α-hydrogen atoms undergo redox reactions resulting in a molecule of the aldehyde reducing to alcohol while another oxidizes to a carboxylic acid salt. This reaction occurs in concentrated alkali solutions.
text_html: Cannizzaro Reaction Aldehydes without α-hydrogen atoms undergo redox reactions resulting in a molecule of the aldehyde reducing to alcohol while another oxidizes to a carboxylic acid salt. This reaction occurs in concentrated alkali solutions.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Chemical Reactions of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Electrophilic Substitution Reactions Aromatic aldehydes and ketones can undergo electrophilic substitution where the carbonyl group acts as a deactivating meta-directing group. Aromatic ketones often react slower than other aromatic compounds in these reactions.
text_html: Electrophilic Substitution Reactions Aromatic aldehydes and ketones can undergo electrophilic substitution where the carbonyl group acts as a deactivating meta-directing group. Aromatic ketones often react slower than other aromatic compounds in these reactions.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones in the Chemical Industry Aldehydes and ketones serve as solvents, starting materials, and reagents for synthesizing various products.
text_html:
Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones in the Chemical Industry
Aldehydes and ketones serve as solvents, starting materials, and reagents for synthesizing various products.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Formaldehyde Applications Known as formalin (40% solution), it is used to: Preserve biological specimens. Prepare bakelite and other polymer products. Manufacture urea-formaldehyde glues.
text_html:
Formaldehyde Applications
Known as formalin (40% solution), it is used to:
  • Preserve biological specimens.
  • Prepare bakelite and other polymer products.
  • Manufacture urea-formaldehyde glues.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Uses of Acetaldehyde Acetaldehyde is a primary material for creating: Acetic acid. Ethyl acetate. Vinyl acetate. Various polymers and drugs.
text_html:
Uses of Acetaldehyde
Acetaldehyde is a primary material for creating:
  • Acetic acid.
  • Ethyl acetate.
  • Vinyl acetate.
  • Various polymers and drugs.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Benzaldehyde Use in Industries Benzaldehyde finds its applications in: Perfumery Dye industries
text_html:
Benzaldehyde Use in Industries
Benzaldehyde finds its applications in:
  • Perfumery
  • Dye industries

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Industrial Solvents: Ketones Acetone and ethyl methyl ketone are prevalent choices for industrial use.
text_html:
Industrial Solvents: Ketones
Acetone and ethyl methyl ketone are prevalent choices for industrial use.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Notable Aldehydes and Ketones for Odours & Flavours Recognized for their distinct scents include: Butyraldehyde Vanillin Acetophenone Camphor
text_html:
Notable Aldehydes and Ketones for Odours & Flavours
Recognized for their distinct scents include:
  • Butyraldehyde
  • Vanillin
  • Acetophenone
  • Camphor

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Formalin Uses Utilized extensively as a protective agent in preserving specimens in scientific settings.
text_html:
Formalin Uses
Utilized extensively as a protective agent in preserving specimens in scientific settings.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Applications of Polymers from Formaldehyde Key polymers derived include phenol-formaldehyde resins and glues.
text_html:
Applications of Polymers from Formaldehyde
Key polymers derived include phenol-formaldehyde resins and glues.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Acetaldehyde in Polymer Production A central component in the synthesis of various polymer materials commonly used in industries.
text_html:
Acetaldehyde in Polymer Production
A central component in the synthesis of various polymer materials commonly used in industries.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Aldehydes and Ketones
text: Scent & Flavor Industry Applications Many aldehydes and ketones contribute to creating compelling fragrances and tastes.
text_html:
Scent & Flavor Industry Applications
Many aldehydes and ketones contribute to creating compelling fragrances and tastes.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Nomenclature: Carboxylic Acids Common names: end with -ic acid e.g. CH 3 CH 2 COOH is propionic acid IUPAC names: replace -e in corresponding alkanes by -oic acid e.g. CH 3 CH 2 COOH is propanoic acid.
text_html:
Nomenclature: Carboxylic Acids
Common names: end with -ic acid e.g. CH3CH2COOH is propionic acid
IUPAC names: replace -e in corresponding alkanes by -oic acid e.g. CH3CH2COOH is propanoic acid.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Carboxylic Acids Overview Carboxylic acids contain a carboxyl functional group, denoted as -COOH. The carboxyl group is a combination of a carbonyl group and a hydroxyl group. These acids can be aliphatic (RCOOH) or aromatic (ArCOOH). They naturally occur, especially as fatty acids in fats as esters of glycerol.
text_html: Carboxylic Acids Overview Carboxylic acids contain a carboxyl functional group, denoted as -COOH. The carboxyl group is a combination of a carbonyl group and a hydroxyl group. These acids can be aliphatic (RCOOH) or aromatic (ArCOOH). They naturally occur, especially as fatty acids in fats as esters of glycerol.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Significance of Carboxylic Acids Carboxylic acids are valuable as they form the base for several organic compounds. Some derivatives include: Anhydrides Esters Acid chlorides Amides
text_html:
Significance of Carboxylic Acids Carboxylic acids are valuable as they form the base for several organic compounds. Some derivatives include:
  • Anhydrides
  • Esters
  • Acid chlorides
  • Amides

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Nomenclature Evolution Since early isolation, many carboxylic acids are identified by common names derived from Latin or Greek. For example: Formic acid from ants Acetic acid from vinegar Butyric acid from rancid butter
text_html:
Nomenclature Evolution Since early isolation, many carboxylic acids are identified by common names derived from Latin or Greek. For example:
  • Formic acid from ants
  • Acetic acid from vinegar
  • Butyric acid from rancid butter

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: IUPAC Naming System In IUPAC, replace "-e" in alkanes with "-oic acid." Number the carboxylic carbon as one. For multiple groups, use prefixes like "di," "tri," alongside the parent chain name.
text_html: IUPAC Naming System In IUPAC, replace "-e" in alkanes with "-oic acid." Number the carboxylic carbon as one. For multiple groups, use prefixes like "di," "tri," alongside the parent chain name.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Table of Names Carboxylic acid examples in Table 8.3 show their common and IUPAC names, highlighting diversity in naming conventions.
text_html: Table of Names Carboxylic acid examples in Table 8.3 show their common and IUPAC names, highlighting diversity in naming conventions.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Carboxyl Group Structure In carboxylic acids, carboxyl carbon bonds are coplanar with an angle of around 120°. It is less electrophilic due to resonance potential.
text_html: Carboxyl Group Structure In carboxylic acids, carboxyl carbon bonds are coplanar with an angle of around 120°. It is less electrophilic due to resonance potential.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Molecular Geometry Bonds in carboxylic acids are in one plane due to resonance. The carboxyl group's geometry affects its chemical reactivity.
text_html: Molecular Geometry Bonds in carboxylic acids are in one plane due to resonance. The carboxyl group's geometry affects its chemical reactivity.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Higher Aliphatic Acids C12-C18 are the fatty acids present in natural fats. These long-chain acids are esterified with glycerol in nature.
text_html: Higher Aliphatic Acids C12-C18 are the fatty acids present in natural fats. These long-chain acids are esterified with glycerol in nature.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Resonance in Carboxyl Group Resonance structures lower electrophilicity in the carboxyl carbon, making it less reactive than typical carbonyl carbon.
text_html: Resonance in Carboxyl Group Resonance structures lower electrophilicity in the carboxyl carbon, making it less reactive than typical carbonyl carbon.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Multiplicative Prefix in Naming When naming compounds with several carboxyl groups, indicate the number with prefixes like dicarboxylic or tricarboxylic acids, ensuring clarity.
text_html: Multiplicative Prefix in Naming When naming compounds with several carboxyl groups, indicate the number with prefixes like dicarboxylic or tricarboxylic acids, ensuring clarity.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Carboxylic Acids Carboxylic acids are carbon compounds with a carboxyl group, -COOH. The carboxyl group includes a carbonyl group attached to a hydroxyl group.
text_html:
Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids are carbon compounds with a carboxyl group, -COOH. The carboxyl group includes a carbonyl group attached to a hydroxyl group.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Types of Carboxylic Acids Carboxylic acids can be: Aliphatic: characterized by the general formula RCOOH Aromatic: characterized by the general formula ArCOOH
text_html:
Types of Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids can be:
  • Aliphatic: characterized by the general formula RCOOH
  • Aromatic: characterized by the general formula ArCOOH

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Occurrence of Carboxylic Acids Carboxylic acids naturally occur in large numbers. Notably, higher aliphatic carboxylic acids (C12 - C18) are known as fatty acids, found in natural fats as glycerol esters.
text_html:
Occurrence of Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids naturally occur in large numbers. Notably, higher aliphatic carboxylic acids (C12 - C18) are known as fatty acids, found in natural fats as glycerol esters.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Uses of Carboxylic Acids They serve as starting material for other organic compounds such as: Anhydrides Esters Acid chlorides Amides
text_html:
Uses of Carboxylic Acids
They serve as starting material for other organic compounds such as:
  1. Anhydrides
  2. Esters
  3. Acid chlorides
  4. Amides

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Nomenclature - Common Names Common names often derive from Latin or Greek using the suffix -ic acid. Examples include: Formic acid from red ants (Latin: formica) Acetic acid from vinegar (Latin: acetum) Butyric acid from rancid butter (Latin: butyrum)
text_html:
Nomenclature - Common Names
Common names often derive from Latin or Greek using the suffix -ic acid. Examples include:
  • Formic acid from red ants (Latin: formica)
  • Acetic acid from vinegar (Latin: acetum)
  • Butyric acid from rancid butter (Latin: butyrum)

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Nomenclature - IUPAC Names In IUPAC naming, replace the -e in alkane with -oic acid. The carboxylic carbon is numbered one.
text_html:
Nomenclature - IUPAC Names
In IUPAC naming, replace the -e in alkane with -oic acid. The carboxylic carbon is numbered one.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Naming Polycarboxylic Compounds For compounds with multiple -COOH groups, use multiplicative prefixes like dicarboxylic or tricarboxylic acid. Indicate -COOH position with numerals.
text_html:
Naming Polycarboxylic Compounds
For compounds with multiple -COOH groups, use multiplicative prefixes like dicarboxylic or tricarboxylic acid. Indicate -COOH position with numerals.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Structure of Carboxyl Group In carboxylic acids, bonds to the carboxyl carbon are planar and approximately 120° apart.
text_html:
Structure of Carboxyl Group
In carboxylic acids, bonds to the carboxyl carbon are planar and approximately 120° apart.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Electrophilicity of Carboxylic Carbon The carboxylic carbon is less electrophilic than carbonyl carbon due to resonance structures.
text_html:
Electrophilicity of Carboxylic Carbon
The carboxylic carbon is less electrophilic than carbonyl carbon due to resonance structures.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nomenclature and Structure of Carboxyl Group
text: Resonance in Carboxylic Group Possible resonance structures involve the carboxylic group, affecting its reactivity and properties.
text_html:
Resonance in Carboxylic Group
Possible resonance structures involve the carboxylic group, affecting its reactivity and properties.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods of Preparation of Carboxylic Acid
text: Preparation of Carboxylic Acids from Primary Alcohols and Aldehydes Primary alcohols can be oxidized using agents like potassium permanganate (KMnO 4 ) or potassium dichromate (K 2 Cr 2 O 7 ). Aldehydes are converted to acids using mild oxidizing agents.
text_html:
Preparation of Carboxylic Acids from Primary Alcohols and Aldehydes
  • Primary alcohols can be oxidized using agents like potassium permanganate (KMnO4) or potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7).
  • Aldehydes are converted to acids using mild oxidizing agents.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods of Preparation of Carboxylic Acid
text: Preparation of Carboxylic Acids from Alkylbenzenes Vigorous oxidation with chromic acid or potassium permanganate is used. The entire side-chain of alkylbenzenes converts to the carboxyl group. Primary and secondary alkyl groups respond well to this oxidation.
text_html:
Preparation of Carboxylic Acids from Alkylbenzenes
  • Vigorous oxidation with chromic acid or potassium permanganate is used.
  • The entire side-chain of alkylbenzenes converts to the carboxyl group.
  • Primary and secondary alkyl groups respond well to this oxidation.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods of Preparation of Carboxylic Acid
text: Conversion using Nitriles and Amides Nitriles are hydrolyzed in the presence of H+ catalyst to form amides and then acids. Mild conditions can halt the reaction at the amide stage.
text_html:
Conversion using Nitriles and Amides
  • Nitriles are hydrolyzed in the presence of H+ catalyst to form amides and then acids.
  • Mild conditions can halt the reaction at the amide stage.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods of Preparation of Carboxylic Acid
text: Use of Grignard Reagents They react with CO 2 to make salts of carboxylic acids. The process ascends the carbon series by one atom.
text_html:
Use of Grignard Reagents
  • They react with CO2 to make salts of carboxylic acids.
  • The process ascends the carbon series by one atom.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods of Preparation of Carboxylic Acid
text: Carboxylic Acid Formation from Acyl Halides Hydrolysis with water yields carboxylic acids. Aqueous base can form carboxylate ions that upon acidification give acids.
text_html:
Carboxylic Acid Formation from Acyl Halides
  • Hydrolysis with water yields carboxylic acids.
  • Aqueous base can form carboxylate ions that upon acidification give acids.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods of Preparation of Carboxylic Acid
text: Preparation from Anhydrides Anhydrides hydrolyzed with water yield corresponding acids.
text_html:
Preparation from Anhydrides
  • Anhydrides hydrolyzed with water yield corresponding acids.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods of Preparation of Carboxylic Acid
text: Esters Conversion to Acids Acidic hydrolysis of esters gives direct formation of carboxylic acids. Basic hydrolysis first provides carboxylates, converting to acids upon acidification.
text_html:
Esters Conversion to Acids
  • Acidic hydrolysis of esters gives direct formation of carboxylic acids.
  • Basic hydrolysis first provides carboxylates, converting to acids upon acidification.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods of Preparation of Carboxylic Acid
text: Role of Oxidizing Agents in Acid Preparation Potassium permanganate in various media oxidizes alcohols and aldehydes. Chromium trioxide with Jones reagent is used for short chain decanol conversion.
text_html:
Role of Oxidizing Agents in Acid Preparation
  • Potassium permanganate in various media oxidizes alcohols and aldehydes.
  • Chromium trioxide with Jones reagent is used for short chain decanol conversion.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods of Preparation of Carboxylic Acid
text: Importance of Alkyl Group Type in Oxidation Primary and secondary alkyl groups oxidize in alkylbenzenes. Tertiary groups remain unaffected by these oxidizing conditions.
text_html:
Importance of Alkyl Group Type in Oxidation
  • Primary and secondary alkyl groups oxidize in alkylbenzenes.
  • Tertiary groups remain unaffected by these oxidizing conditions.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Methods of Preparation of Carboxylic Acid
text: Utilizing Ascending Series in Organic Chemistry Grignard and nitrile methods add a carbon atom compared to alkyl halides. It's a common technique for carbon chain expansion in organic synthesis.
text_html:
Utilizing Ascending Series in Organic Chemistry
  • Grignard and nitrile methods add a carbon atom compared to alkyl halides.
  • It's a common technique for carbon chain expansion in organic synthesis.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Carboxylic Acid
text: Methanoic Acid Applications Utilized in rubber and textile industries. Applied in dyeing and leather processing. Involved in electroplating procedures.
text_html:
Methanoic Acid Applications
  • Utilized in rubber and textile industries.
  • Applied in dyeing and leather processing.
  • Involved in electroplating procedures.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Carboxylic Acid
text: Uses of Ethanoic Acid & Hexanedioic Acid Ethanoic acid acts as a solvent. Known as vinegar in the food industry. Hexanedioic acid is essential for nylon-6, 6 production.
text_html:
Uses of Ethanoic Acid & Hexanedioic Acid
  • Ethanoic acid acts as a solvent.
  • Known as vinegar in the food industry.
  • Hexanedioic acid is essential for nylon-6, 6 production.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Uses of Carboxylic Acid
text: Benzoic Acid & Fatty Acids Applications Esters from benzoic acid serve the perfumery industry. Sodium benzoate works as a food preservative. Higher fatty acids are key in producing soaps and detergents.
text_html:
Benzoic Acid & Fatty Acids Applications
  • Esters from benzoic acid serve the perfumery industry.
  • Sodium benzoate works as a food preservative.
  • Higher fatty acids are key in producing soaps and detergents.
